coredata.dc:description: Developing new hybrid materials is critical for addressing the current needs of the world in various fields, such as energy, sensing, health, hygiene, and others. C-dots are a member of the carbon nanomaterial family with numerous applications. Aggregation is one of the barriers to the performance of C-dots, which causes luminescence quenching, surface area decreases, etc. To improve the performance of C-dots, numerous matrices including metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent-organic frameworks (COFs), and polymers have been composited with C-dots. The porous crystalline structures, which are constituents of metal nodes and organic linkers (MOFs) or covalently attached organic units (COFs) provide privileged features such as high specific surface area, tunable structures, and pore diameters, modifiable surface, high thermal, mechanical, and chemical stabilities. Also, the MOFs and COFs protect the C-dots from the environment. Therefore, MOF/C-dots and COF/C-dots composites combine their features while retaining topological properties and improving performances. In this review, we first compare MOFs with COFs as matrices for C-dots. Then, the recent progress in developing hybrid MOFs/C-dots and COFs/C-dots composites has been discussed and their applications in various fields have been explained briefly.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing new hybrid materials is critical for addressing the current needs of the world in various fields, such as energy, sensing, health, hygiene, and others. C-dots are a member of the carbon nanomaterial family with numerous applications. Aggregation is one of the barriers to the performance of C-dots, which causes luminescence quenching, surface area decreases, etc. To improve the performance of C-dots, numerous matrices including metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent-organic frameworks (COFs), and polymers have been composited with C-dots. The porous crystalline structures, which are constituents of metal nodes and organic linkers (MOFs) or covalently attached organic units (COFs) provide privileged features such as high specific surface area, tunable structures, and pore diameters, modifiable surface, high thermal, mechanical, and chemical stabilities. Also, the MOFs and COFs protect the C-dots from the environment. Therefore, MOF/C-dots and COF/C-dots composites combine their features while retaining topological properties and improving performances. In this review, we first compare MOFs with COFs as matrices for C-dots. Then, the recent progress in developing hybrid MOFs/C-dots and COFs/C-dots composites has been discussed and their applications in various fields have been explained briefly.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) are a member of the carbon nanomaterial family with a size below 10 nm [1,2]. Due to their low toxicity [3], affordability [4], excellent optical properties [5–7], and biocompatibility [8,9], C-dots have gained attraction in different research fields such as electrocatalysis [10,11], photocatalysis [12], light emitting diode [13,14], energy conversion [15–17], wastewater treatment [18], sensors [19,20], biotechnology [21], and bioimaging and theragnostic [22,23]. C-dots have been identified as core-shell structures, with the core being either amorphous (a mixture of sp2/sp3) or graphitic crystalline (sp2), based on the carbon source used and the prevalence of sp2 carbon within the core [24,25]. Generally, there are four types of C-dots (CDs) with different structures including graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) (Fig. 1 ) [26,27]. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are a specific class of graphene-based materials with zero-dimensional (0D) nature. These dots consist of one or a few layers of graphene, with their lateral dimension typically being <30 nm. The GQDs have exceptional crystallinity due to the presence of a honeycomb lattice structure [28]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) typically exhibit a spherical morphology, characterized by a higher number of graphite layers in the central carbon core compared to graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The primary difference between GQDs and CQDs lies in the fact that GQDs are comprised of single or a few graphene sheets that exhibit anisotropic properties, featuring lateral dimensions that exceed their thickness. Consequently, the level of exfoliation plays a pivotal role in dictating whether the ultimate product manifests as CQDs or GQDs. CNDs are characterized by a significant carbonization degree and the presence of specific chemical groups on their surface [29]. However, they do not typically exhibit a clear crystal lattice structure or polymer features. The main feature of carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) is their carbonized core and polymeric surface structure [30,31]. While most of the C-dots have spherical morphology, other geometrical configurations have been developed such as triangular [32,33] and hexagonal [34] shapes.

All types of C-dots are typically synthesized using “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches [35,36]. The fragmentation of graphite, graphenes, carbon nanotube, and active carbons using high-energy methods is a defining feature of the top-down approach. Different techniques including arc discharge [37], laser ablation [38], electrochemical method [39], hydrothermal/solvothermal [40], and chemical exfoliation [41] have been used in top-down method for fabrication of diverse types of C-dotst. On the other hand, the bottom-up method involves the synthesis of C-dots from molecular or polymeric percursors usisng combustion [42], microwave [43], pyrolysis [44], hydrothermal [45], and solvothermal [46] strategies. The synthesis of C-dots is faced with several challenges including aggregation, size manipulation, and surface functionality/properties [47]. The electronic and photoluminescence properties of carbon dots are greatly influenced by their size, a phenomenon known as quantum confinement effects. It is important to highlight that the optical and electronic attributes of C-dots are dictated by factors such as morphology, surface chemistry, and dimensions [48]. Therefore, various strategies have been devised to produce homogeneous and uniform C-dots with adjustable fluorescence emission, such as modifying synthesis conditions, pH levels, solvents, concentrations/types/ratios of precursor materials, post-treatment processes, and purification or separation techniques [25]. C-dots have numerous functional groups on the surface such as carbonyl/carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, ether, and amine groups [49,50]. The manipulation of surface properties through modification techniques is essential in regulating the surface traits of C-dots [51,52]. This enables them to exhibit valuable imaging properties such as high quantum yield, stability, and low toxicity. Moreover, surface modification imparts desirable biosensing properties to C-dots, including high selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy. Depending on the nature of functional groups present on the surface of C-dots, diverse surface functionalization techniques have been utilized, such as coordination chemistry, covalent conjugation, π-π interactions, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and sol-gel methods (Fig. 2 ) [53,54].

Aggregation is one of the main issues of C-dots which deteriorates their properties such as luminescence quenching and surface area reducing [55]. To overcome these problems, C-dots (as a guest) have been embedded or anchored into various host matrices such as zeolites, mesoporous material, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), polymers, and nano-carriers [56–58].

The coordination between metal ions as nodes and organic molecules as linkers gives rise to the formation of crystalline porous structures known as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [59–61]. Owing to their privileged features including high specific surface area, tunable structure and pore diameters, and modifiable surface [62,63], MOFs have revealed exquisite potential in various fields. Moreover, the function, structure, and architecture of MOFs can be engineered via in situ or post-synthesis approaches [64–66]. Several MOF derivatives including mechanically interlocked molecules into MOFs [67], fluorinated-MOFs [68], guest@MOF composites [69,70], cyclodextrin MOFs [71,72], hierarchically porous MOFs [73], bimetallic MOFs [74,75], pyrene-based MOFs [76], porphyrin-based MOFs [77], perovskites/MOFs hybrids [78,79], chiral MOFs [80], highly connected building unit based-MOFs [81], hard-templated MOFs [82], two-dimensional (2D) MOFs [83–85], hollow MOFs [86], and MOFs with low valent metal nodes [87] have been reported. These MOFs have found applications in catalysis [88–90], photocatalysis [91,92], separation [93,94], water treatments [95,96], energy storage [97,98], gas storage [99], ion conduction [100,101], sensors [102,103], food science [104], and different medical and biomedical fields such as antibacterial [105], antitumor [106], drug delivery [107,108], phototherapy [109], and biological [110] applications. Various complementary techniques including different spectroscopies [111,112], molecular modeling [113,114], gravimetric and mass balance [115,116], and adsorption-based methods [117] have been applied for the characterization and investigation of these substances. The main drawbacks of MOFs are their low physiochemical stability [118,119], mechanochemistry [120], and toxicity [121]. The fully organic analogous of MOFs, covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) are another class of porous polymeric matrices, which are composed of covalently attached organic bulding blocks [122–124]. Similar to MOFs, they possess incredible characteristics such as high surface area, highly ordered and predictable structures with good stability [125,126]. Numerous synthetic strategies have been developed for preparation and enhancement the physiochemical characteristics of COFs [127–129]. Similar to MOFs the function, structure, and topology of COFs can be engineered via post-modifications [130], processing [131,132], and supramolecular design [133,134]. COFs are generally classified in two-dimensional (2D) layered structures [135,136] and three-dimensional (3D) networks [137,138]. Despite high stability, the preference of MOFs over COFs is the presence of the metal ion which endow many physiochemical properties to MOF structures. Hence, metal-COFs as a bridge between MOFs and COFs have been reported [139,140]. Additionally, ionic covalent-organic frameworks (iCOFs) which include cationic and anionic COFs [141–143], and magnetic COFs (MCOFs) composites [144] as other members of the COFs family have been reported. The application of COFs in catalysis [145,146], photocatalysis [147,148], electrocatalysis [149,150], energy storage [151,152], rechargeable batteries [153,154], solar fuels [155], ion conduction [156,157], environmental [158,159], separation [160,161], sensors [162,163], and biomedical [164] have been explored.

There are some review papers about C-dot/MOFs composites for specific applications. Yan et al. [165] and Li et al. [166] reviwed comprehensivly the application of C-dot/MOF in sensors and discussed different sensing mechanisms including photo-induced electron transfer, inner filter effect, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, static & dynamic quenching, and aggregation-caused quenching. Li et al. reviewed photocatalysis and the sensing of environmental pollutants by C-dot/MOF composites [167]. In another similar review, the typical synthesis methods of C-dot/MOF and their potential applications in photocatalysis and sensing have been reviewed by Zhang et al. [168]. C-dots/porous materials (i.e., MOF and COF) composites combine the advantages of C-dots with high porosity of COF/MOF substances while maintaining pristine morphology. In addition, these composites demonstrated favorable physiochemical features due to the reciprocal restitution and the synergistic consequence of their pristine substance's advantages and disadvantages. For instance, as a selective sensor, fluorescence quenching of C-dots due to the aggregation is omitted via combination with porous substances, and various functional groups on the surface of C-dots provide binding sites for selective targets [169]. The C-dots can enter the pores of these substances because of their small size and affinity or can be grafted onto the surface of porous materials. Therefore, numerous strategies and approaches have been developed for the synthesis of these composites such as encapsulation, impregnation, pore-confined, post-modification, in-situ encapsulation, one-pot, etc.

In this review, we concentrate on the recent progress in the fabrication of C-dots/MOF and C-dot/COF hybrid composites between 2015 and 2023. Hence, the advantages and drawbacks of MOF and COF are discussed and compared in the first step. Then, in comparison to available review papers about C-dot/MOF, we have sorted C-dot/MOF composites based on their central metal, and on the other hand, their applications are widely evaluated. Additionally, the fabrication and main applications of C-dot/COF composites are discussed for the first time in this review. There are some gaps and disadvantages in C-dot/MOF composites that can be addressed by the fabrication of the C-dot/COF analogous.

Although both MOFs and COFs show high specific surface area, tunable structure and pore diameters, and modifiable surface, there are specific differences between them. COFs due to the presence of light elements usually exhibit lower densities than MOFs. Highly crosslinked structures in COFs by covalent bonds enhanced stability in vigorous acidic, basic, and redox conditions [170]. The absence of metal elements in COFs reduces their catalytic activity [171], however, various strategies have been developed for introducing metals into the structure of COFs to address their challenge [172,173]. Both MOFs and COFs have good biocompatibility and have been used on the MOF/COF-based nanozymes [174], however, because of the presence of metal ions, MOFs exhibit higher toxicity than COFs [175]. The MOFs are more hydrophobic than COFs which causes better performance in applications such as the removal of dyes or hazardous materials from water. However, post-modifications can further improve the potential application of COFs in water treatment [176]. Beyond some differences, MOFs and COFs complement each other and their applications for membranes [177,178], CO2 conversion [179], and metal-ion batteries [180] have been reported.

Nowadays, the interactions between MOF and C-dots as two new generations of materials have been at the center of attention by many researchers. MOFs can be used as a self-sacrificed template for the synthesis of C-dots. For instance, porous carbon and nitrogen-doped C-dots have been synthesized from an anionic MOF by Bhattacharyya and colleagues [181], or nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N@C-dots) have been fabricated from MOF (ZIF-8) by Xu and coworkers [182]. Also, Wu et al. [183] reported a sensor based on red C-dot and zirconium MOF (UiO-66-(COOH)2) for the detection of Cu2+ ions in an aqueous solution. Herein, Cu2+ acted as a conjunction agent between C-dots and MOF. The simulatenous coordination of Cu2+ with the carboxylate groups of MOF and amino groups of C-dots lead to the color change in the hybrid system. Electrocatalysts based on nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes (N@CNTs) have been developed throughout pyrolysis of C-dots/MOF(ZIF-67) at 600 °C for the reduction of oxygen [184]. Utilizing C-dots modified Co3O4/In2O3 composite, derived from MOF/MOF(ZIF-67/In-MIL-68), as a photocatalyst presents an economically viable option for CO2 reduction [185]. The CO generation rate by this C-dots photocatalyst was 761 times higher compared to the Ru photocatalyst, primarily due to the lower band gap of the C-dots (1.6 eV) in comparison to the Ru complex (2.4 eV). As mentioned previously, C-dots and MOFs have their own unique privileged features which can preserve these features in the C-dots/MOF composites. The following section provides a comprehensive review of diverse C-dot/MOF composites, categorized based on the metal center of the MOF segment. These composites consist of C-dot/Zn-MOF, C-dot/Zr-MOF, C-dot/Eu-MOF, C-dot/Fe,Ni-MOF, and C-dot/other MOF, all of which exhibit unique applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General strategies for fabrication of C-dots@MOF composites
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication techniques for C-dots@MOF composites can be classified into four main groups, which are in situ C-dot synthesis, in situ MOF synthesis, in situ C-dot/MOF synthesis, and impregnation method as illustrated in Fig. 3 .

In the in situ C-dot synthesis, the precursors of C-dots are loaded into the MOF pores and then converted into C-dots upon heating. The dimensions of the C-dots utilized in this approach closely match the pore size of the MOF, indicating the potential for controlling the size of C-dots. Loading of glucose intro the different MOFs (including MIL-53(Fe), HKUST-1, ZIF-8, and MIL-101) and subsequent heating at 200 °C lead to the in situ formation of encapsulated C-dots with size 1.5–3.2 nm which was corresponding to the MOF pores sizes [186,187]. On contrary, in the in situ MOF synthesis strategy, MOF is prepared from its precursors in the presence of C-dots. Dissolution of 2-methylimidazole and zinc acetate or zinc nitrate, as MOF precursors, in the presence of C-dots lead to the formation of ZIF-8 MOF around C-dots [188,189]. In the in situ C-dot/MOF synthesis approach, precursors of MOF and precursors of C-dots are mixed together and then converted into C-dot/MOF hybrid under certain conditions. Mixing powder forms of ZrOCl2⋅8H2O and 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid ligand (as MOF precursors) with tetraethylammonium bromide (as C-dot precursor) by mortar and pestle and then heating in autoclave at 180 °C under hydrothermal process lead to the in situ formation of C-dots encapsulated into UiO-66(OH)2 MOF [190]. Finally, in the impregnation strategy, pre-fabricated C-dots are encapsulated into the pre-fabricated MOF through simple physical mixing. Immersing UiO-66 nanocrystals in water in the presence of C-dots and stirring for 12 h lead to the diffusion of the C-dots inside the MOF pores [191]. Similarly, mixing a dispersion of ZIF-8 in methanol with a dispersion of glucose-derived C-dots in water, followed by 30 min sonication and 12 h stirring at room temperature produced the C-dots/ZIF-8 composite through impregnation method [192].

The low toxicity of zinc and its wide-range application qualify it for fabrication of Zn-based MOFs for sensing, biosensing, and biomedical applications. The organic ligand, emission, and applications of C-dots/Zn-MOF composites are summarized in Table 1 .

One of the main application of Zn-based MOFs are for sensing of metal ions. Accordingly, Wu et al. have prepared a yellow C-dots-linked ZIF-8 (yC-dots/ZIF-8) fluorescent composite as a sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions in the aqueous environment [193]. This composite exhibited aqueous stability, excellent selectivity, and sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.3690 μM. Fe3+ ions could selectively quench the fluorescence of yC-dot/ZIF-8 composite in the presence of other ions such as Zn2+, Ba2+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Cr3+, Mn2+, and Cu2+. Guo et al. developed a C-dots/Eu3+ doped ZIF-8 (C-dots/Eu@ZIF-8) nanocomposite as a dual emission fluorescence sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions in the linear range 0–6 μM with detection limit of 0.897 μM [194]. Fe3+ ions could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in the presence of other ions including Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+, Cr2+, Na+, Mg2+, and Zn2+. The gold nanoparticle/C-dots/ZIF-8 (Au/C-dots/ZIF-8) nanocomposite as a dual fluorescence emission was prepared via a one-pot method and applied for the detection of Cu2+ [195]. Cu2+ ions could selectively quench the fluorescence emission of Au nanoparticles without affecting the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. The Au/C-dots/ZIF-8 ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibited good sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.3324 nM and a linear range of 10−3-103 μM. Ma and associatess synthesized a dual-emission fluorescent sensor by encapsulating hybrid CdTe/CdS/ZnS quantum dots and C-dots into the MOF framework (QD/C-dots/ZIF-8) for ratiometric detection of Cu2+ in water samples [196]. The CdTe/C-dots/ZIF-8 composite not only possesses the advantages of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor (due to the high fluorescence properties of the CdTe and C-dot), which eliminates the most variation by the instrument features and instability of the source light intensity but also enrichment the target analytes by adsorption in ZIF-8 frameworks. This ratiometric sensor exhibited a linear range of 5–100 nM with a detection limit of 1.53 nM. In another work, a C-dot/ZIF-8 composite was utilized as a dual-emitting platform for the detection of Cu2+ [197]. The C-dots were synthesized via the hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and then dispersed into Zn(NO3)2 and dimethylimidazole mixture using ultrasonic, thus C-dots/ZIF-8 was prepared by the one-pot method. The C-dots/ZIF-8 composite exhibited quantum yields of 7%, a fluorescence lifetime of 2.79 ns, and a detection limit of 11.712 μM for Cu2+ ions. In another work, branched poly-(ethylenimine)-capped C-dots (BPEI-C-dots) encapsulated into ZIF-8 (BPEI-C-dots/ZIF-8) composite was prepared for detection of Cu2+ in water samples [198]. BPEI-C-dots/ZIF-8 composite combines the advantages of the BPEI-C-dots (fluorescent activity and selectivity) and ZIF-8 (accumulate target analytes), therefore demonstrating a very low detection limit of 80 pM.

C-dot/MOF hybrids can additionally be utilized for dual detection of two metal ions with high sensitivity. Fluorescence-enhanced triple-emission C-dots/2-methylimidazole-Zn luminescence MOF (C-dots/MIM-Zn LMOF) with down/up-conversion dual channels turn off-on was developed for the detection of metal ions and glutathione (GSH) in real blood samples [199]. The C-dot/MIM-Zn was prepared using a C-dot-induced dual ligand co-assisted strategy, in which C-dots and MIM act as a dual-ligand to connect with Zn2+ ion. The C-dots/MIM-Zn LMOF showed Stoke and anti-Stoke type excitation profiles at 370 nm and 790 nm, and the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with a detection limit of 20 nM and 30 nM, respectively. Interentingly, the Fe3+@CDs/MIM-Zn composit could detect GSH in blood samples the range of 0.5–70 μM with the detection limit of 15 nM. Fan et al. [200] synthesized C-dots/MOF composite as a fluorescence on-off sensor for sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in an aqueous solution. This C-dots/MOF composite showed low toxicity with a detection limit of 2.3 ppb and 1.3 ppb for Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. The C-dots activate the fluorescence intensity of the MOF due to an enhanced conjugate chain of π electrons via hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl group on the surface of the C-dots and the amino groups of MOF. However, in the presence of metal ions (Fe3+ and Cu2+) because of the chelation of metal ions with the amino groups of MOF, C-dots are segregated from MOF and would cause quenching of the fluorescence intensity of C-dots/MOF.

C-dot/MOF composites demonstrate exceptional sensitivity as sensors for detecting trace amounts of heavy and toxic metals in biological and environmental fluids at low concentrations. Guo et al. synthesized a gold nanoparticle/blue C-dots/ZIF-8 nanocomposite as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ions [201]. Indeed, Hg2+ could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of Au nanoparticles in the presence of other ions including NH4 +, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, and Cr3+, while the fluorescence intensity of C-dots remains unchanged. The Au/C-dots/ZIF-8 nanocomposite showed good stability, selectivity, and sensitivity with a detection limit of 1 nM and a linear range of 3–30 nM. A magnetic Fe3O4@carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) core and ZIF-8 shell embedded with C-dots (Fe3O4@CMC/C-dots/ZIF-8) composite was prepared via hydrothermal method and in situ synthesis as a multifunctional hybrid sorbent for detection and removal of uranium (VI) [202]. The composite with C-dots exhibited higher adsorption capacity (173.60 mg/g) than the one without C-dots (96.02 mg/g) due to the presence of the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the loaded C-dots. Also, the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@CMC/C-dots/ZIF-8 composite at pH 4 is maximum due to lower competition between the proton and U(VI) ions. However, at higher pH, more complex forms of the U(VI) formed and the adsorption capacity was decreased. Thus, the Fe3O4@CMC/C-dots/ZIF-8 composite showed the maximum U(VI) adsorption of 606.06 mg/g at pH 4 and T = 298 K following the Langmuir model. The flurosence of the C-dot/MOF composite could be quenched by increasing the concentration of U(VI) in the range of 10–100 ppm.

Furthermore, aside from utilizing fluorometry for the detection of metal ions, the C-dots/MOF composites have the capability to identify traces amounts of organic compounds such as antibiotics, vitamins, polyheterocyclic aromatics, and organic pollutants. For instance, a gold nanoparticle/electroreduced C-dots/Zn-MOF (Au/C-dots/MOF) composite was prepared via a one-pot electrodeposition approach as an electrochemical sensor for detection of bisphenol A in real plastic samples [203]. The gold nanoparticle and C-dots act as conductive agents to improve the conductivity of MOFs, which enhances the sensor performance. The Au/C-dots/MOF composite showed a linear range of 0.07–1.3 μM with a detection limit of 0.032 μM. In another work, nickel & nitrogen-doped C-dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles embedded into the ZIF-8 (Fe3O4/Ni,N@C-dots/ZIF-8) nanocomposite was developed as a magnetic fluorimetric nanosensor for the detection of fenitrothion in food samples [204]. Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Ni,N@C-dots act as separating agents for the target analyte enrichment and sensitive substance for producing fluorescence signals, respectively. This nanosensor showed a detection limit of 0.8 nM with a linear range of 0.0009–0.12 μM. The gold nanoparticle/green emitted C-dots/ZIF-8 (Au/C-dots/ZIF-8) composite was utilized as a dual fluorescence emission probe for the detection of cephalexin (CFX) residues in the milk samples [205]. The CFX could selectively quench the fluorescence emission of Au nanoparticles in the presence of other substances including alanine, glycine, tryptophan, glucose, lactose, and sucrose, while the fluorescence emission of C-dots remains unchanged. The Au/C-dots/ZIF-8 ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibited good sensitivity and selectivity with a detection limit of 0.04 ng/mL. A fluorescence sensor based on the molecularly imprinted polymer with a core-shell structure (MIP/C-dots/ZIF-8) has been prepared via the sol-gel method for the detection of malachite green (MG) [206]. The fluorescence intensity of the MIP/C-dots/ZIF-8 nanocomposite remarkably quenched with the addition of MG in the presence of other ions including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, NH4 +, Ag+, Cd2+, Br−, S2O3 2−, H2PO−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, S2−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, and Cl−. MIP/C-dots/ZIF-8 nanocomposite demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity with a detection limit of 2.93 nM with a linear range of 20–180 nM. The yellow-emitted C-dots and blue-emitted Cu nanoparticles embedded in ZIF-8 (YC-dots/Cu@ZIF-8) nanocomposite was applied as a ratiometric probe for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) [207]. This nanocomposite demonstrated a detection limit of 1.56 μM with a linear range of 4–22 μM. The fluorescence intensity of the YC-dots could remarkably quench in the presence of AA while the blue fluorescence of Cu nanoparticles remains unchanged. The concentration of quercetin could be detected in a linear range of 0.01–50 μM with detection limit 3.5 nM using a C-dots/ZIF-8 composite [189]. The ZIF-8 framework acts as an adsorbent for the enrichment of analyte targets while the C-dots act as selective and sensitive tentacles between the analyte and ZIF-8. By combination of C-dots/ZIF-8 with molecularly imprinted polymer and developing of MIP/C-dots/ZIF-8 composite the linear range for quercetin detection was further increased to 0–50 μM and detection limit reduced to 2.9 nM [208].

Bao and coworkers reported a C-dots/2D-MOF fluorescent probe for the determination of dipicolinic acid (DPA), anthrax biomarker, with a detection limit of 7 nM [209]. The as-prepared probe exhibited good selectivity to DPA in the presence of other interfering substances including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, glucose, tyrosine, lysine, arginine, benzoic acid, and p-phthalic acid because of selective coordination between catechol groups of DPA and Zn2+ of Zn-MOF. Similarly, dopamine, another molecule with catechol unit, could be detected using the of C-dots/ZIF-8 composite as a fluorescent sensor in a linear range of 0.1–200 μM with a detection limit of 16.64 nM [210]. Indeed, dopamine is pre-concentrated in the pores of ZIF-8, and then via the basic sites in ZIF-8 is oxidized to dopamine-quinone which can be adsorbed on the ZIF-8 framework using π-π interaction. Finally, electrostatic interaction between dopamine-quinone and C-dots could quench the fluorescence emission of C-dots. A blue and yellow-emitted C-dots/ZIF-8 (BYC-dots/ZIF-8) composite was prepared in a one-pot method and applied as a ratiometric probe for the detection of glutathione [211]. The coordination interaction between the residual o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and Cu2+ ions causes fluorescence intensity enhancement of the yellow-emitted C-dots and slight quenching of the blue-emitted C-dots fluorescence. In the presence of glutathione due to the strong interaction between Cu2+ and glutathione, the fluorescence of both C-dots was gradually restored. The BYC-dots/ZIF-8 composite exhibited a detection limit of 0.90 nM with a linear range of 3–25 nM for glutathione.

Nitrogen-doped C-dots/isoreticular MOF-3 (N@C-dots/IRMOF-3) was utilized as a luminescent probe for selective sensing of trinitrotoluene (TNT) explosive with a detection limit of 17.07 ppb [212]. The formation of π-complex between the benzene rings of nitro aromatic compounds and the amino groups of MOF could remarkably quench the luminescence of IRMOF-3. Therefore, N@C-dots not only increase the number of the amino groups on the single IRMOF-3 but also by the formation of a protecting layer on the surface of the IRMOF-3 inhibit the interference from another nitro aromatic substance including dinitrotoluene (DNT), and para-nitrophenol (p-NP).

C-dot/MOF hybrid can be utilized as a sensor for sensing other hazardous materials as well. For instance, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles and dual-emission rhodamine B modified C-dots embedded into the ZIF-8 (RhB/Fe3O4/C-dots/ZIF-8) nanoprobe was developed using the one-pot method as a magnetic and fluorescent ratiometric sensor for HClO sensing [213]. HClO could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the Fe3O4@CMC/C-dots/ZIF-8 composite in the presence of other substances including H2O2, OH−, NO3 −, NO2 −, NO, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and urea. Also, the nanoprobe possesses some combined advantages such as good sensitivity and selectivity, stability in aqueous solution, magnetic separation, and enrichment of the analyte.

C-dots/MOF hybrids have also found applications in diverse medical fiels. For instance, as biocidal agents, as drug delivery systems, and as sensing platforms for monitoring some tissue or cell funtions. Vitamin B12-loaded carbon dot/mixed-ligand MOF encapsulated within the gelatin microsphere (GMS) was developed for pH monitoring and in vitro wound healing assessment (Fig. 4a) [214]. This composite exhibited pH-dependent fluorescence intensity due to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface carboxyl and amine groups. At pH 8, deprotonation of the carboxylic groups of the C-dots increased the formation of the delocalized π bonds and “n” electrons, and render the generation of a new surface state with a lower band gap. Therefore, the maximum fluorescence intensity was at pH 8. On contrary, the full proponation of the amino groups of gelatin at pH < 4 lead to the fluorescence quenching. Additionally, strong biocidal activity of this composite which can prevent the wound infection and slow release of vitamin B12 which can increase the proliferation of resident fibroblast L929 cells qualify it as a suitable candidate for wound healing applications. Ma and coworkers developed a biomimetic pH-responsive ZIF-8 carrier for targeted delivery of carbon nanodot-SOD nanozyme (SOD = superoxide dismutase) and clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR)/CRISPR-associated protein 9 (Cas9) (CRISPR/Cas9) system for site-specific treatment of ulcerative colitis (Fig. 4b) [215]. The composite was further protected from immune system of the body by camouflaging with macrophage membrane. The nanoparticle was directed toward the inflammatory sites with the guidance of the macrophage membrane. Upon the acidic environment of inflammation, the nanozymes and plasmids were released following the breakdown of ZIF-8. The synergistic action of C-dot nanozymes and the CRISPR/Cas9 system effectively reduced the inflammation associated with ulcerative colitis.

A nanoprobe for pH-sensitive targeted anticancer drug delivery was developed by employing a composite consisting of C-dots embedded magnetic nanoparticles@chitosan@MOF composite (Fig. 5a) [216]. Briefly, the Fe3O4 was coated with carboxymethyl chitosan (OCMC), then further modified by coating with folic acid (FA) encapsulated MOF. The surface of the obtained Fe3O4@OCMC@FA/MOF was further decorated by chemical conjugation with C-dots. This hybrid nanoprobe integrates multiple key characteristics. For instance, it showed a prolonged release of doxorubicin (DOX) in a pH-sensitive manner due to the presence of chitosan. The Fe3O4 component serves as a contrast agent for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), enhancing the hybrid's ability to deliver drugs through magnetic guidance. The fluorescent C-dots render the bioimaging capability to the nanoprobe. The gold nanoparticles/C-dots/ZIF-8 (Au/C-dots/ZIF-8) nanocomposite was utilized as an electrochemiluminescence sensor for monitoring cell apoptosis [217]. The formation of gold nanoparticles in the pores of ZIF-8 accelerates converting the C-dots to C-dots•- (anion-radical) which would cause enhancement of the sensor signal. Therefore, the ZIF-8 framework not only acts as a carrier but also as an accelerator of the process. A hierarchical Zn-MOF confined with CsPbBr3 perovskite quantum dots and aminated C-dots (PeQDs/aminated C-dots/MOF) suprastructure was utilized for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) bioanalysis [218]. The hierarchical Zn-MOF offered a trustworthy barrier to protect the PeQDs and the aminated C-dots from the environment, which enhanced their stability. The abundant number of amines on the surface of the aminated C-dots improved the charge transfer and conductivity performance of hierarchical Zn-MOF. Also, the ECL efficiency of the PeQDs in the presence of aminated C-dots was improved. This PeQD/C-dots/MOF composite exhibited potential for evaluating the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of T4 polynucleotide kinase (T4 PNK) activity which T4 PNK is known to have a vital function in DNA repair and cellular metabolism.

In addition to the sensing of metals, organic molecules, and medical applications the Zn-based MOFs have found application in other different fields. For instance, Ozyilmazand et al. showed that presence of C-dots increase the activity and stability of Candida rugosa lipase (CRL) encapsulated in ZIF-8 [219]. Xu et al. utilized a one-step hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of room-temperature phosphorescences (RTP) C-dots as a guest and its incorporation into a Zn-MOF (as host) for 4D coding applications (Fig. 5b) [220]. This nanocomposites demonstrated room temperature phosphorescence with an adjustable emission wavelength ranging from blue (478 nm) to red (631 nm), and the lifetime could be adjusted from 85.67 to 1064.21 ms. The MOF provided a rigid environment to prohibit the nonradiative losses from the triple transition and promote the intersystem crossing (ISC) efficiency due to the presence of metal ions. Therefore, MOF acts as an RTP activator of fluorescent C-dots including both the emission wavelength and lifetime, and can remarkably modulate RTP features of C-dots from 437 to 525 nm and 1.17 to 2.62 ns, respectively. Han et al. synthesized a nitrogen-doped C-dots embedded MOF on ZnO (ZnO/N@C-dots/ZIF-8) nanotube for photoelectrochemical water splitting [221]. The ZnO/N@C-dots/ZIF-8 showed enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting due to ordered N@C-dots embedded in ZIF-8 on the ZnO nanotube, which in turn facilitated the electron pathway and increased the light outcome range.

Zirconium, as a high oxidation state cation, can create strong coordination with MOFs ligands. Therefore, the zirconium-based MOFs (Zr-MOFs) are more stable in organic solvents and water than other metal-MOFs. In this section, we summarize recent advances in C-dots/Zr-MOF composites. The organic ligands, emissions, and applications of C-dots/Zr-MOF composites are summarized in Table 2 .

Composites of C-dots with Zr-based MOFs can be used for sensing of heavy metals. For instance, the gold nanoparticles/C-dots/Zr-MOF (Au/C-dots/NH2-UiO-66) composites have been applied for the fluorescence detection of Pb2+ ions in water (Fig. 6a) [222]. The fluorescence intensity of Au/C-dots/NH2-UiO-66 with 1.4 wt% Au content could be quenched by Pb2+ ions and demonstrated a detection limit of 80 ppt. However, with increasing the Au content (3.7 and 5.2 wt%) the fluorescence intensity of Au/C-dots/NH2-UiO-66 in the presence of 2 ppb Pb2+ ions increased because the metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) promoted by large Au nanoparticle (> 20 nm). Thus, the large Au nanoparticles would cause light scattering and fluorescent enhancement. The preparation of encapsulated C-dots into the Zr-MOF (C-dots/UiO-66) using a solvent-free approach as a multifunctional fluorescent sensor has been reported for the monitoring of ambiance temperature, pH of aqueous solutions, and the detection of Fe3+ ions [190]. The Fe3+ ions could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of C-dots/UiO-66 in the presence of other ions such as Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, Mn2+, K+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, and Cd2+ through a collisional quenching process. The C-dots/UiO-66 composite showed a quantum yield (QY) of 2.27%, a detection limit of 0.76 μM for Fe3+ ions, linear response to the pH and temperature from 3.0 to 7.0 and 25–110 °C, respectively.

The Zr-based MOF in combination with C-dots have utilized as highly sensitive sensors for fluorescence detection of traces of organic compounds. A green C-dots/UiO-66 (gC-dots/Zr-MOF) composite was prepared through a solvothermal method and used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing norfloxacin (NFX) in milk and pork samples [223]. The NFXs are adsorbed on the surface of gC-dots/UiO-66 via hydrogen bond and in turn, increase the electrostatic repulsion between gC-dots/UiO-66 particles which cause diminish the non-radiative decay and fluorescence enhancement. The fluorescent intensity of the gC-dots/UiO-66 composite remarkably enhanced with the addition of NFX in the presence of other substances including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, glutamic acid, histidine, cysteine, tryptophan, amoxicillin, ampicillin, erythromycin, chloramphenicol, and tetracycline. Therefore, the gC-dots/UiO-66 composite exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.082 μM in a linear range of 1–8 μM. Zhang et al. synthesized C-dots/UiO-66(COOH)2 (a Zr-MOF) using the hydrogen bond self-assembly method as a multifunctional fluorescent sensor for the detection of chlortetracycline (CTC) [224]. Additionally, it dispalyed a remarkable stimuli-response in fluorescence intensities toward pH and temperature changes qualifying it for monitoring the pH and temperature changes. The CTC molecule could selectively quench the fluorescence of C-dots/UiO-66(COOH)2 composite in the presence of other substances including gibberellin, erythromycin, cysteine, glutamate, aspartic acid, ascorbic acid, chloramphenicol, tetracycline, doxycycline hydrochloride, and oxytetracycline through inner filter effect (IFE) process. The C-dots/Zr-MOF sensor showed a detection limit of 0.102 μM with a linear range of 0–100 μM for the detection of CTC, also a linear response to the pH from 2.0 to 9.0 and temperature from 10 to 80 °C with a relative temperature sensitivity of 0.82% °C−1. Li et al. prepared C-dots/Zr-MOF composites via defect-containing luminescent UiO-66 type MOF (named UiO-66 N) self‑carbonization through solvothermal reaction for sensing of picric acid [225]. The crystal defect of UiO-66 N would cause the departure and rearrangement of C-dots, thus facilitating self‑carbonization and luminescence changing from blue to green of C-dots upon calcination. The C-dots/Zr-MOF composite demonstrated a high quenching constant of 4.0 × 105 M−1 with a detection limit of 0.65 μM. A fluorescent amine-functionalized C-dots/UiO-67 (amine-C-dots/UiO-67) was prepared via a post-modification strategy and applied as a sensor for selective and sensitive detection of 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) [226]. UiO-67 act as an adsorbent for the enrichment of selective analyte targets while the C-dots act as a selective and sensitive functional monomer for the detection of DNP. The DNP could remarkably quench the fluorescence intensity of amine-C-dots/UiO-67 in the presence of other substances and ions including Ag+, K+, Na+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Co2+, NH4 +, phenol, 4-nitrophenol, and trinitrophenol. The amine-C-dots/UiO-67 sensor showed a detection limit of 5.2 nM for DNP. Similarly, an amine-C-dots immobilized into UiO-66 (amine-C-dots/UiO-66) composite via post-synthetic modification strategy was prepared and utilized as a fluorescent sensor for sensitive and selective detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) with a linear range of 0.01–20 μM and a detection limit of 3.5 nM (Fig. 6b) [191]. The fluorescence intensity of amine-C-dots/UiO-66with the addition of 4-NP in the presence of other substances including Ag+, K+, Na+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Co2+, NH4 +, phenol, dinitrophenol, and trinitrophenol was remarkably quenched. A dual-mode fluorescent sensor by in-situ encapsulation of nitrogen-doped C-dots into a Zr/porphyrin-based MOF (named as PCN-222) was prepared for the detection of glyphosate (GLP) [227]. The fluorescent intensity of N@C-dots/PCN-222in the presence of GLP via photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process was remarkably quenched. Indeed, the GLP could form a complex by coordination with the surface groups of N@C-dots/PCN-222 that would cause non-radiative emission. Thus, N@C-dots/PCN-222 sensor displayed a detection limit of 9.06 μg/L in the range of 0.01–6.67 mg/L.

Zr-based MOF in combination with C-dots have been utilized as platforms for diverse medical applications. For instance, incorporation of C-dots into the nanopores and the surface of porphyrinic porous Zr-based MOFs (named as PCN-224) and then decorating the surface of the hybrid with mitochondria-targeted moieties of triphenylphosphines endow the formation of a composite for mitochondria-targeted photodynamic therapy (Fig. 7a) [228]. The C-dots act as a near IR (NIR) laser receiver for transferring the energy to the PCN-224 MOF which causes the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) to kill the cancer cells and induced apoptosis. This composite exhibited good biocompatibility and anticancer activity. A bimetallic ZrHf-based MOF integrated with C-dots has been employed as a highly sensitive platform for the early detection of HER2 and HER2-overexpressed cancer cells (Fig. 7b) [229]. The C-dots/Zr,Hf-MOF provided a stable platform due to strong interaction between the phosphoric group of the aptamer tentacles and zirconium through Zr-O-P bonds which promote the stability of aptamer/HER2 complex. Also, the C-dots with the electrostatic interaction improve the binding ability of the aptamer tentacles and enhance the electrochemical activity of the aptasensor. This aptasensor exhibited a detection limit of 19 fg/mL and 23 cell/mL with a linear range of 0.001–10 ng/mL and 1 × 102–1 × 105 cell/mL for HER2 and MCF-7 cell, respectively. In another work, a dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescence thermometer was prepared by encapsulating C-dots and Eu3+ into Zr-MOF (MOF-867) [Eu/C-dots/MOF-867] via simple post-modification route for physiological temperature sensing [230]. The sensor displayed low cell toxicity and linear response to the temperature from 20 to 60 °C with a relative temperature sensitivity of 1.81% K−1.

The C-dot/Zr-MOF composites have found other miscelanous applications as well. For instance, the synthesize of C-dots/NH2-UiO-66 using both C-dots decorated on the NH2-UiO-66 surface and encapsulated into the NH2-UiO-66 strategy has been reported for photocatalytic CO2 reduction [231]. The C-dots not only act as photosensitizers but also due to the heterojunction between C-dots and ZrO clusters in the NH2-UiO-66 result in fast charge separation and transfer. The encapsulated C-dots/NH2-UiO-66 demonstrated higher photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity than decorated C-dots/NH2-UiO-66. Wang et al. prepared blue-emitting C-dots embedded into the yellow-emitting Zr-MOF (C-dots/Zr-MOF) nanocomposite with the photoluminescence quantum yields of 37% (in solid state) as white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) [232]. The down-conversion WLEDs displayed a luminous efficiency of 1.7 lm/W and a color-rendering index (CRI) of 82. Fabrication of C-dots/Zr-MOF composite through coordination between carboxyl groups of C-dot with Zr4+ ions of MOF-802 (a model of MOF proton conductors) was used as simple strategy to improve the water-retention capacity and proton conductivity [233]. The acidic groups (-COOH and –SO3H) of the C-dots possessed more mobile protons, also the coordination of C-dots with Zr4+ ions suppressed the release of water from C-dots/Zr-MOF that in turn improved the proton conductivity. Therefore, the C-dots/Zr-MOF exhibited enhanced water-retention capacity with proton conductivity of 1.13 × 10−1 S cm−1 at room temperature and 98% relative humidity (RH).

Rare-earth (RE) metals involving the lanthanide (Ln) series as well as scandium and yttrium have a privileged electronic structure and high coordination number due to the unfilled 4f electron shell. Therefore, rare-earth MOFs (RE-MOFs) have attracted the attention of scientists for different applications due to the unique features of the RE along with MOFs. In this section, we discuss the recent advances in C-dots/Eu-MOF composites. The organic ligands, emissions, and applications of C-dots/Eu-MOF composites are summarized in Table 3 .

The composites of C-dots with Eu-based MOFs have widely been used for sensing of metal ions. A dual-emission fluorescence probe was fabricated by encapsulating the blue C-dots into the Eu-MOF (C-dots/Eu-MOF) for the simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and Cu+ (Fig. 8a) [234]. The Cu+ ion could selectively quench the fluorescent emission of C-dots via the formation of C-dots-Cu+ by the inner filter effect (IFE), whereas the Cu2+ could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots/Eu-MOF by replacing of the Eu3+ with Cu2+ ions. The C-dots/Eu-MOF nanocomposite demonstrated a fast response (<1 min), detection limits of 0.22 μM and 0.14 μM with linear range of 0.5–20 μM for Cu+ and Cu2+, respectively.

A ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on blue-emitted nitrogen and cobalt(II) co-doped C-dots embedded into the Eu-MOF (N,Co@C-dots/Eu-MOF) was prepared for the detection of Cr(VI) [235]. The fluorescence intensity of the N,Co@C-dots selectively quenched in the presence of the Cr(VI) ions due to the inner filter effect (IFE) while the Eu-MOF acted as an adsorbent for the enrichment of analyte targets and the fluorescence emission of Eu-MOF remains unchanged. The N,Co@C-dots/Eu-MOF probe showed a detection limit of 0.21 μM with a linear range of 2–100 μM. Hao et al. fabricated a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor using C-dots embedded into the Eu-2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) MOF (C-dots/Eu-DPA MOF) for the detection of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 8b) [236]. The fluorescence intensity of the Eu-DPA MOF remarkably quenched in the presence of the Cu2+ ions due to strong coordination between Cu2+ and the DPA which causes replacing of the Eu3+ with Cu2+ ions, while the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots remains unaffected. Also, the Cu2+ ions could selectively quench the fluorescence emission of the Eu-DPA MOF in the presence of other substances including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, and NH4 +. The C-dots/Eu-DPA MOF sensor showed a detection limit of 26.3 nM with a linear range of 50–1 × 104 nM. Guo et al. synthesized a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe by encapsulating the C-dots into Eu-MOF (C-dots/Eu-MOF) through an in-situ approach and utilized as a sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ions in tap water samples (Fig. 8c) [237]. The Hg2+ could selectively quench the fluorescence emission of the C-dots in the presence of other ions including Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+, Cr3+, Na+, Mg2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, and Zn2+ while the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOF remains unaffected. However, the Eu-MOF enhanced sensitivity of the C-dots/Eu-MOF sensor, thus the C-dots/Eu-MOF demonstrated a detection limit of 0.12 nM in a linear range of 0–300 μM. Embedding formamide-derived N-doped carbon dots (FA-CDs) in the cavities of Eu(III)-based MOFs afford a suitable sensor for selective detection of Ag+ ions [238]. In the presence of Ag+ ions, the strong coordination between CN groups of the C-dots and Ag+ would cause quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots via the static quenching process. Also, Ag+ ions could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the N@C-dots/Eu-MOF ratiometric sensor in the presence of other ions including K+, Na+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Al3+, and Fe3+. Therefore, N@C-dots/Eu-MOF hydrogel showed high sensitivity and selectivity with a detection limit of 80 pM in a linear range of 0.3–100 nM.

The synergistic integration of C-dots and Eu-based MOFs exhibits promising capabilities for detecting organic compounds in biological fluids, thus highlighting their significant potential in various medical applications. Fei et al. synthesized a blue-emitted C-dots covalently grafted onto red-emitted Eu-MOF (bC-dots/Eu-MOF) composite as a platform for ratiometric sensing of biomarker 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA) in human urine samples [239]. The bC-dots were grafted on the Eu-MOF surface by the complexation between the bC-dots–COOH and the Eu ion of Eu-MOF, which in turn resulted in quenching the blue emission of bC-dots. However, upon the addition of 5-HIAA to bC-dots/Eu-MOF the bC-dots are released and showed strong blue emission, while the red emission of Eu-MOF owing to grafted with 5-HIAA is quenched via thermal relaxation. Also, the blue emission of bC-dots/Eu-MOF could be selectively recovered with the addition of 5-HIAA in the presence of other substances including Na+, urine, thioglycolic acid, aspartic acid, cysteine, glycine, methionine, and glucose. The bC-dots/Eu-MOF sensor exhibited a detection limit of 0.3 μM for 5-HIAA in a linear range of 0.3–70 μM. A dual-emission fluorescent blue-emitted C-dots encapsulated into the Eu-MOF (C-dots/Eu-MOF) ratiometric sensor was prepared via in-situ self-assembly strategy for the detection of calcium pyridine dicarboxylate (CaDPA) as a fast and sensitive approach to the diagnosis of anthrax spore biomarkers [240]. The coordination between Eu3+ and carboxyl groups and pyridinium nitrogen of the CaDPA caused energy transfer from CaDPA to the Eu3+ and enhancement of Eu-MOF luminescence. Also, the CaDPA could selectively improve the fluorescent intensity of the C-dots/Eu-MOF in the presence of other substances including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, L-phenylalanine, D-aspartic, glutamine, methionine, L-cysteine, glycine, benzoic acid, o-phthalic acid, and m-phthalic acid. This selective C-dots/Eu-MOF probe demonstrated a linear range of 8–170 μg/L with a detection limit of 0.66 μg/L. Another dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe was prepared by incorporating the blue-emitted C-dots into the Eu-MOF (bC-dots/Eu-MOF) for the detection of doxycycline antibiotic and MnO4 − ions [241]. In the presence of doxycycline, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots is quenched due to the energy transfer between doxycycline and C-dots while the formation of Eu3+/doxycycline complex would cause fluorescence enhancement of the Eu-MOF. Doxycycline could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of C-dots and enhance the fluorescence emission of the Eu-MOF in the presence of other substances including chloramphenicol, kanamycin, erythromycin, isoniazid, neomycin, phenylalanine, valine, cysteine, glutathione, aspartic acid, glycine, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, and metronidazole. There are two possible fluorescence quenching mechanisms for the bC-dots/Eu-MOF in the presence of MnO4 − ions. First, the energy competition of excitation and absorption between Eu-MOF and MnO4 − ions would cause the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the bC-dots/Eu-MOF. Second, the weak interaction of the MnO4 − ions and Eu-MOF results in the reaction of MnO4 − ions with C-dots and converting to the MnO2 which causes enhancement in the interaction between MnO4 − ions and Eu-MOF. The MnO4 − ions could selectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the bC-dots/Eu-MOF sensor in the presence of other ions including NO3 −, OAc−, CO3 2−, ClO3 −, C2O4 2−, SO3 2−, NO2 −, H2PO2 −, and HCO3 −. The bC-dots/Eu-MOF showed a detection limit of 0.36 μM and 0.68 μM in a linear range of 0–60 μM and 0–100 μM for doxycycline and MnO4 − ions, respectively.

C-dot/Eu-based MOFs have found utility in various other applications, including but not limited to energy-related domains and the detection of a diverse array of analytes. For instance, Han et al. fabricated the white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) by self-quenching-resistant N,P-doped C-dots combined with Eu-MOF (N,P@C-dots/Eu-MOF) [242]. The N,P@C-dots were prepared by a solvothermal approach using polyethyleneimine (PEI), that N,P@C-dots surrounded by PEI tentacles and inhibit the direct π–π interaction between particles. Thus, preventing the fluorescence quenching via aggregation-induced phenomenon. The incorporation of blue-emitted nitrogen and sulfur co-doped C-dots into red-emitted Eu-MOF (N,S@C-dots/Eu-MOF) using an in-situ approach affords a dual-emission fluorescent composite for reversible anti-counterfeit ink and detection of water in organic solvents [243]. The luminescence of blue N,S@C-dots, and red Eu-MOF depended on the water and organic solvents, respectively. The red-emission of Eu-MOF in the presence of hydrochloric acid vapor was completely quenched, however, in the fuming triethylamine the emission was recovered. A dual-emission ratiometric probe using blue-emitted nitrogen and sulfur co-doped C-dots encapsulated into the red-emitted Eu-MOF (N,S@C-dots/Eu-MOF) was prepared for the detection of water in organic solvents with the response range of 0.2–30% and a detection limit of 0.03% (Fig. 9 ) [244]. In the presence of water the N,S@C-dots could be released and cause fluorescence enhancement of the N,S@C-dots, while the red emission of the Eu-MOF due to the energy transfer between Eu3+ and coordinated OH groups was quenched.

Transition metal ions including nickel and iron have good hardness, corrosion resistance, low toxicity, thermal and electrical conductivity. Thus, Fe and Ni-MOFs have attracted the attention of researchers for different applications because of the privileged features of these elements along with MOFs. However, pristine Ni-MOFs are unstable in aqueous solutions and Fe-MOFs have lower conductivity than Ni-MOFs. In this section, we summarize recent advances in C-dots/Fe and Ni-MOF composites. The organic ligand, emission, and application of C-dots/Fe and Ni-MOF composites are summarized in Table 4 .

Same as other C-dots/MOF composites, the Ni-based and Fe-based MOFs in combination with C-dots have been used for sensing of metal ions and organic compounds. For instance, Ni-MOF functionalized nitrogen-doped C-dots (N@C-dots/Ni-MOF) was used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA) [245]. The Fe3+ ions could remarkably quench the fluorescence intensity of N@C-dots/Ni-MOF due to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between Fe3+ and N@C-dots/Ni-MOF in the presence of other ions including Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+ and Fe2+. However, with the addition of the AA, the fluorescence intensity of N@C-dots/Ni-MOF was restored probably due to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ by AA. This sensor demonstrated a detection limit of 0.0098 μg/mL and 0.0876 μg/mL with a linear range of 0.029–8.00 μg/mL and 0.263–18.00 μg/mL for Fe3+ and AA, respectively. Another fluorescent composite was prepared by incorporating amine-C-dots into Fe-MOF (amine C-dots/Fe-MOF) for selective detection of 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) [246]. The Fe-MOF framework acts as an adsorbent for the enrichment of analyte targets and the C-dots act as a selective substance between the analyte and Fe-MOF. The 6-MP could significantly quench the fluorescence intensity of amine C-dots/Fe-MOF through the inner filter effect in the presence of other substances including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, sucrose, vitamin C, L-Cys, cholesterol, and creatin. This sensor showed a detection limit of 0.3273 nM with a linear range of 0.98–5.88 nM. An enantioselective electrode was prepared via embedding chiral C-dots into Ni-MOF coated on the surface of copper foam (CuF) (C-dots/Ni-MOF/CuF) and then utilized as a sensor for detection of penicillamine (PA) enantiomers [247]. The chiral C-dots improved the electron transport from the electrode. The C-dots/Ni-MOF/CuF electrode displayed a linear relationship between the L-PA% and current along with good selectivity and sensitivity. This system could recognize the chiral penicillamine enantiomers in the linear range 100 to 600 μM.

Hybridization of Fe- and Ni-based MOFs provide excellent materials for drug delivery and cell imaging applications. Bai et al. have fabricated a nanoplatform by self-assembly of near-infrared emission C-dots and Fe-MOF (C-dots/Fe-MOF) through hydrothermal methods for tumor microenvironment (TME)-activated cancer imaging and chemodynamic photothermal combined therapy (Fig. 10a) [248]. C-dots were prepared from glutathione (GSH) via the solvothermal method, then assembled with Fe-MOF which causes quenching of the fluorescence intensity of C-dots through aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon. When C-dots/Fe-MOF reaches the TME, the GSH (the reducing agent producing in cancer cells) reduces Fe3+ in Fe-MOF to Fe2+ which causes a collapse of C-dots/Fe-MOF assembly and release of C-dots and Fe2+ ions. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of released C-dots is restored and could be used for cancer imaging. Also, the Fenton reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2 produces hydroxyl radicals (•OH) for chemodynamic therapy. In addition, light irradiation of TME with a near-infrared laser generated more hydroxyl radicals and improved the performance of chemodynamic therapy. Lin et al. fabricated a fluorescent nanocomposite by incorporating C-dots into Fe-MOF core and O-carboxymethyl chitosan (OCMC) shell (OCMC@C-dots/Fe-MOF) for pH-responsive drug delivery and dual-mode imaging (Fig. 10b) [249]. The Fe-MOF not only acts as a platform with a large surface area for loading drugs but also can be used for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) due to the presence of Fe3+ ions. Also, fluorescent C-dots have been used for fluorescence imaging. The hydrogen bond between the -OH and -NH2 surface groups of OCMC and doxorubicin (DOX) at pH 7.4 would cause the loading of a drug. The protonation of these groups at pH 3.8 (an acidic condition in tumor cells) causes the release of DOX. Therefore, OCMC@C-dots/Fe-MOF nanocomposite exhibited low cytotoxicity, high drug loading (50 mg/g), and pH-responsive behavior for drug delivery.

C-dot/MOF composites have versatile applications in various energy-related fields. The incorporating C-dots into Ni-MOF provide an efficent catalyst for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [250]. The composite showed an overpotential of 320 mV with a Tafel slope of 62 mV dec−1 and stability after 1000 cycles with high retention of current density after 40 h. Another electrocatalyst based on carboxylated C-dots induced bimetallic MOF (carboxylated C-dots/Fe and Ni-MOF) was prepared for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) (Fig. 11a) [251]. The carboxylated C-dots promote the positive charge on the active sites due to the electron-withdrawing –COOH groups and enhanced the OER performance of C-dots/Fe and Ni-MOF. Therefore, carboxylated C-dots/Fe and Ni-MOF demonstrated an overpotential of 261 mV, a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with high stability after 48 h, and a Tafel slope of 56 mV dec−1. The C-dots/Ni-MOF composite prepared via wetness impregnation approach and utilized as an electrocatalyst for ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR) [252]. The C-dots enhance not only the adsorption of ethanol on the surface of C-dots/Ni-MOF but also the charge transfer. The composite displayed a current density of 119 mA cm−2 with a retention of 90% after 10,000 s, a Tafel slope of 93.9 mV dec−1, and an exchange current value of 2.06 × 10−5 A cm−2. Pan et al. have synthesized C-dots/Ni-MOF composite by one-pot hydrothermal route and utilized it as cathode for assembly of high-performance supercapacitors (Fig. 11b) [253]. The C-dots not only enhanced the electronic conductivity of Ni-MOF but also modify the structure of Ni-MOF for disclosing further active sites. Thus, the fabricated composite exhibited a specific capacitance of 2457.9 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, and capacitance retention of 84.5% after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. Incorporation of C-dots into Fe-MOF as a template and decoration with Bi2S3 (C-dots/Fe-MOF/Bi2S3) afford a photocatalyst for dye degradation [254]. This catalyst was used for reducing 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol and degradation of dyes including methylene blue and acid blue 92. The Fe-MOF act as a carrier for C-dots and Bi2S3 and the synergistic effect of these two agents caused a faster reduction and higher yield. The C-dots/Fe-MOF/Bi2S3 nanocomposite displayed a reduction time of 240 s with 60% and 75% degradation for methylene blue and acid blue 92, respectively.

Composites of C-dots with other metals (Cu, Cd, Ag, Cr, Al, and In) have also been fabricated for various other applications. In this section, we summarize recent advances in C-dots/other metal-MOF composites. The organic ligand, emission, and application of C-dots/other metal-MOF composites are summarized in Table 5 .

All of the other C-dot/MOF nanocomposites have widely been used in detecting of traces amount of metal ions and organic compounds in environmental and biological fluids. The Eu3+/C-dots/Al-MOF was used as a dual-emission sensor for the detection of Hg2+ [255]. The Hg2+ ion selectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of C-dots while the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ ions remained unchanged. This sensor showed good selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ with a detection limit of 13 nM and a linear range of 0–150 μM. Embedding of into Cd-based MOFs following be further incorporating of Eu3+ ions afford a sensor for sensing of Fe3+, p-nitrotoluene and 2,4-dinitrophenol in water solutions with detection limits of 0.034, 0.062 and 0.0111 μM, respectively [256]. A ratiometric fluorescent probe was prepared by embedding C-dots and Eu3+ ions into Al-MOF (Eu3+/C-dots/MIL-53-COOH) for the detection of diaminotoluene (TDA) in urine [257]. The TDA selectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of the doped-Eu3+ ions while the fluorescence intensity of C-dots remained intact. The Eu3+/C-dots/MIL-53-COOH sensor exhibited a detection limit of 6.8 μg/mL with a linear range of 0–3 mM. Decoration of hydroxyapatite nanowire by C-dots and Tb-MOF results in the HApNWs/C-dots/Tb-MOF composite as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of dopamine [258]. The dopamine significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence of other interfering substances. The HApNWs/C-dots/Tb-MOF sensor displayed good selectivity along with a detection limit of 12.26 nM with a linear range of 0.04–20 μM. A fluorescent sensor for the detection of chloramphenicol (CAP) was fabricated by the decoration of silver-MOF with C-dots via self-assembly at room temperature [259]. The CAP significantly could quench the fluorescence intensity of C-dots/Ag-MOF through the photoinduced electron-transfer (PET) mechanism. The fluorescence intensity of C-dots/Ag-MOF was selectively quenched in the presence of other substances including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, SO4 2−, NH4 +, CH3COO−, Al3+, Cl−, phenylalanine, valine, glucose, tryptophan, ornithine, carnitine, vitamin, fructose, serine, cysteine, aspartic acid, and tyrosine. Thus, the C-dots/Ag-MOF sensor exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity with a detection limit of 44 nM in a linear range of 2–80 μM. Encapsulation of blue emission C-dots into Cr-MOF afford bC-dots/Cr-MOF as a fluorescent probe for the detection of uric acid [260]. The uric acid remarkably quenched the fluorescence intensity of bC-dots/Cr-MOF via the inner filter effect process. The as-prepared bC-dots/Cr-MOF nanocomposite demonstrated high stability and selectivity with a detection limit of 1.29 μM and a linear range of 20–45 μM. In another work, a dual-emission sensor by C-dots embedded into the Cu-MOF (C-dots/Cu-MOF) through the coordination between Cu2+ ions and -COO− groups of C-dots for the determination of pesticide thiophanate-methyl (TM) was fabricated [261]. The coordination interaction between the C-dots and Cu2+ ions quenched the C-dots' fluorescence due to the charge transfer effect. However, the presence of TM lead to the gradual restoring of the fluorescence owing to the strong interaction between Cu2+ and TM. This C-dots/Cu-MOF composite demonstrated a detection limit of 3.67 nM with a linear range of 30.7–769 nM for TM. A C-dots/Cu-MOF nanotube was prepared as a sensor for the detection of pyrophosphatase (PPase) activity [262]. The fluorescence intensity of C-dots in C-dots/Cu-MOF was weak due to the static quenching effect. The C-dots/Cu-MOF was decomposed in the presence of pyrophosphate ion (PPi) owing to coordination between Cu2+ ions and PPi leading to the restoration of the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. However, hydrolysis of the PPi to phosphate ion (Pi) in the presence of the PPase and weak affinity between Cu2+ ions and Pi would not cause the decomposition of C-dots/Cu-MOF. The C-dots/Cu-MOF probe exhibited a detection limit of 0.03 mU/mL with two linear ranges of 0.1–5 mU/mL and 5–50 mU/mL. Encapsulation of the red emission C-dots and Tb3+ ions into In-MOF produced the Tb3+/C-dots/In-MOF composite as a red-green dual emission ratiometric probe for the detection of relative humidity (RH) and water content in organic solvents [263]. The fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence of water was quenched through the agglomeration effect of C-dots, while the fluorescence intensity of green emission was enhanced. Therefore, Tb3+/C-dots/In-MOF showed a detection limit of 0.28% with a linear range of 0–30% and 33%–85.1% for water content and RH, respectively.

In addition to the sensing, the C-dot/MOF hybrids are excellent candidates in energy storage materials. For instance, Meng et al. have prepared CdS and C-dots co-encapsulated into Cr-MOF (CdS/C-dots/MIL-101) for photocatalytic H2 evolution [264]. The C-dots act as an electron collector and enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CdS/C-dots/Cr-MOF. This CdS/C-dots/Cr-MOF demonstrated the H2 evolution rate of 14.66 μmol/h which was 18.6 times higher than that of neat CdS. The incorporation of C-dots and stearic acid (SA), respectively, as a fluorescent and thermal energy guest into Cr-MOF as a host led to the formation of SA/C-dots/Cr-MOF hybrid structure for phase change materials (PCMs) (Fig. 12a) [265]. Phase change materials act as the primary latent thermal energy storage medium, allowing them to absorb extra thermal energy from the environment and release it through a phase transition as needed. The SA enhanced the thermal stability of SA/C-dots/Cr-MOF by hydrogen bond interaction with surface groups of Cr-MOF. However, the strong hydrogen bond interaction restricted the free movement of SA chains and reduced the latent heat. However, the addition of C-dots would weaken the hydrogen bonds between SA and Cr-MOF and increases the latent heat. Therefore, the SA/C-dots/Cr-MOF demonstrated good thermal stability, durability, thermal conductivity of 0.41 W m−1 K−1, and red-green-blue emissions.

The potential of C-dot/MOF hybrids extends beyond their current applications. These hybrids can be further investigated for their efficacy as catalysts in various chemical transformations. Additionally, they hold promise as fertilizers in agricultural practices. By exploring these alternative applications, we can unlock the full potential of C-dot/MOF hybrids. For example, anchoring of carboxymethyl cellulose-derived C-dots into the Cu-based MOFs provide a nanofertilizer as a nutrient for the sunflower plant [266]. The C-dots/Cu-MOF would cause enhancing the chlorophyll, carotenoid content, and morphological features of the sunflower.

Immobilization of diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (DABCO) ionic liquid into magnetic C-dots, followed by conjugation with Cu-MOF (IL/Fe3O4/Cdots/Cu-MOF) produce a highly active catalyst for the preparation of tetrahydrobenzo[b]pyrans under mild condition (50 °C, 60 min) (Fig. 12b) [267]. In another work, a nitrogen and sulfur-doped C-dots/Ag-MOF (N and S@C-dots/Ag-MOF) hybrid was fabricated as an antibacterial nanocomposite [268]. The high affinity of Ag in Ag-MOF to sulfur compounds in the extracellular locations causes the decomposition of Ag-MOF into Ag° and Ag2S substances with strong biocidal activities.

Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) as organic analogs of MOFs are produce from organic units by strong covalent bonds [269,270]. With excellent properties such as high thermal and chemical stabilities, low densities, high porosity with tunable pore sizes, COFs have found wide applications in different areas such as gas adsorption, catalysis, extraction and separation, and energy storage materials [271,272]. COFs are more biologically compatible than metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) since they do not have metal ions in their structure [164,273]. Therefore, COFs are better suited for encapsulating organic fluorescence molecules for environmental and in vivo detections. C-dots as highly fluorescent molecules are good candidates for hybridizing with COFs. There are generally two groups of COF-C-dots hybrid materials. In the first group, the carbon dots are simply encapsulated into the COF structure usually by loading into the pores of COFs whereas in the second group, the carbon dots are covalently grafted to the C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulating the C-dots in the COF structure
ce:section.ce:para: In this strategy, C-dots are usually loaded into the pores of COFs. Encapsulation of N-doped-C dots and rhodamine B (RhB) in the pores of TAPT/DHTA-based COF lead to the formation of an “on-off” ratiometric fluorescence for Hg2+ detection in water (Fig. 13a) [274]. COF with pore size 3.67 nm was prepared through imine formation between 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl) triazine (TAPT) and 2,5-dihydroxyl-terephthalaldehyde (DHTA) in the presence of RhB. H-bonding and acid-based interaction between RhB and COF could fix the RhB in the COF structure. Under 350 nm excitation, individual C-dots and RhB showed emission peaks at 438 nm (blue emission) and 575 nm (red emission), respectively which both of these emissions were observed with slight shifts in C-dots/RhB@COF composite with purple emission (Fig. 13b). The presence of Hg2+ quenched the fluorescence of COF but increased the fluorescence of RhB (Fig. 13c). This probe could selectively in the presence of other metal ions detect Hg2+ with linear range 0.048–10 μM and detection limit 16 nM.

Reverse micro-emulsion of a mixture of magnetic COFs and C-dots using TEOS and APTES monomers in the presence of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) template afford a selective MIP fluorescent sensor for TNP detection in different types of real water samples [275]. Magnetic COF was prepared by condensation polymerization of 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene with 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde through imine formation in the presence of Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. 14 ). This composite combines the advantages of the magnetic effect of Fe3O4 for magnetic separation, the fluorescent effect of C-dots, and the selective and accumulation effect of adsorbents. The saturation adsorption capacity for TNP was 166.85 mg/g. The TNP could remarkably quench the fluorescence intensity of the composite via the static quenching process in the presence of analogous organic molecules and ions. Practically, this sensor showed a detection limit of 0.1 nM with a linear range of 0.0003–100 μM for detection of TNP in water samples. Using a similar reverse micro-emulsion strategy, another magnetic COF/C-dot molecularly imprinted composite with bisphenol A template was prepared for optosensing of bisphenol A in foods [276]. The blue fluorescence emission of this system was quenched selectively by bisphenol A through charge transfer mechanism and therefore, it could detect bisphenol A with a detection limit of 12 μg/L and a linear range of 25–3000 μg/L.

Encapsulating of C-dots inside 1,3,5-tris(4-formylphenyl)benzene (TFB)/2,5-dihydroxyterephthalohydrazide (DHTH) covalent organic frameworks (TDCOFs) and then surface modification of the COFs by poly(N-isoproylacrylamide) (PINPAM) provide a thermo-responsive fluorescence probe for detection of pyrethroids, a group of commercial pesticides [277]. COF was prepared by formation of hydrazone bonds among TFB and DHTH and catechol-terminated PNIPAM could attach to the surface of COF through coordination interactions. While the fluorescence intensity of C-dots@COF remained unchanged from 24 to 40 °C., the fluorescence intensity of C-dots@COF@PNIPAM was decreased by increasing temperature due to the covering of the surface of the sensor by phase-transition-induced PNIPAM layer. This sensor could detect pyrethroids based on an ON-OFF pathway. At temperatures below LCST (<35 °C), hydrophilic structure of PNIPAM allowed the pyrethroids to achieve the CDs@TDCOFs, representing “on” state; but at T > LCST, hydrophobic PNIPAM create a barrier layer and prevent pyrethroids to reach the label CDs@TDCOFs centers; representing the “off” state. Through quenching the fluorescence of CDs@TDCOF@PNIPAM in the presence of pyrethroids, this probe showed a detection limit of 0.69 μg/L with a linear range 5–400 μg/L for recognizing of pyrethroids.

In addition to utilizing COF/CD systems for determination the organic analytes, the COF-CD composites can also be applied for the detection of heavy metal ions since the heavy metals can affect the fluorescence intensity of COF-CD system. Encapsulating N-doped C-dots inside the pores of COFs (from condensation of benzotrithiophene tricarbaldehyde with terephthalic dihydrazide through imine bonds) produce dual-emission fluorescence 2D nanosheets which could be used as a sensor for Fe3+ detection [278]. The N@C-dots not only induced COF self-exfoliated into the two-dimensional nanosheet due to reduces interlayer accumulation and attenuation of interlaminar π-π interactions but also improve the fluorescence intensity of COF via fluorescence resonance energy transfer by prohibiting the intramolecular rotation. At 300 nm excitation, this C-dot/COF hybrid system could emit two fluorescence peaks at 450 and 547 nm, respectively, related to C-dots ad COF. Quenching the fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe3+ ions, owing to the internal filtration effect, make it a suitable sensor for iron ions detection with a detection limit of 3.40 nM with a linear range of 10.21 nM to 24 μM. Doping C-dots into COF (from 1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol and p-phenylenediamine) through reverse microemulsion (using TESO/APTES) provides a fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions in tap water [279]. In this sensor, COF act as an adsorbent for the enrichment of analyte and C-dots act as selective and sensitive fluorescent tentacles for Hg2+ detection. The fluorescence of the sensor was significantly and selectively quenched by Hg2+ ions through the charge transfer effect in the presence of other metal ions. With a linear range of detection 10–100 μg/L, a detection limit of 0.75 μg/L, high adsorption capacity (235 mg/g), rapid adsorption kinetic (58 mg/g), and high recyclability this C-dot/COF composite was an excellent candidate from removal and detection of Hg2+ in water. Similarly, the synthesis of COF from 2,4,6-tris(4-aminophenyl)-1,3,5-triazine (TAPT) and thieno[3,2-b]thiophene-2,5-dicarboxaldehyde (TT) in the presence of N-doped C-dots lead to the in-situ encapsulation of C-dots inside the COF pores providing a fluorescent sensor for “on-off” detection of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 15a) [280]. This encapsulation induced self-exfoliating of COF layers and formation of 2D nanosheets with much higher colloidal stability. The C-dots/COF systems emitted a strong fluorescence signal at 370 for C-dots and a weak fluorescence signal at 445 nm for COF. The weak fluorescence signal of COF is due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between monomer parts of COF. By addition of low concentration of Cu2+ (0–10 μM), binding of Cu2+ ions to the C-dots quenched the fluorescence of C-dots whereas the fluorescence of COF remained unchanged (Fig. 15b). In a linear range of 51.9 nM to 0.75 μM with detection limit 17.3 nM copper ions could be detected. However, by further increasing the concentration of Cu2+ (18.0–38.5 μM) the fluorescence signal related to COF was increased much higher than the fluorescence signal related to C-dots (Fig. 15c). In this way, copper ions could be detected in a linear range of 18.0–26.0 μM Cu2+. Indeed, at this range of concentration, Cu2+ coordinates with S and N elements of the COF and inhibit the PET process which lead to restoring the fluorescence of COF (Fig. 15d).

The conversion of CO2 into valuable chemicals has been considered as an effective method for reducing the greenhouse content of the atmosphere [281]. Photocatalytic CO2 reduction is of the widely applied methods for CO2 utilization [282].
