The well-defined porosity, high surface area and tunable structure of COFs make them excellent platforms for CO2 photo-reduction. Metalloporphyrins are excellent catalysts with high activity for CO2 photo-reduction [283]. Direct incorporation of metalloporphyrin molecules into the channels of COFs has the problem of leaching out these small molecules and reducing the catalytic activity. Porphyrin-based carbon dots are prepared and applied for different applications [284]. C-dots are among the excellent guests for COF materials. Accordingly, a COF hosting metalloporphyrin-based C-dots was prepared by Zhong et al. and applied as a heterogeneous catalyst for CO2 photo-reduction [285]. As presented in Fig. 16a, COF was prepared by condensation among 2,4,6-triformylphloroglucinol and 2,6-diaminobenzo[1,2-d:4,5-d’]bisthiazole through imine formation and then metalloporphyrin-based C-dots were grow inside the pores of COF by selective pyrolysis of glucose. Because of the lower energy barrier for the adsorption of CO2 on Ni@C-dots (0.27 eV) than H+ (1.3 eV), the M@C-dots/COF photocatalyst has high selectivity of up to 98% for the reduction of CO2 to CO over H2 generation (Fig. 16b,c). Additionally, this catalyst showed superior recyclable stability indicating high stability of in situ prepared C-dots inside the COF pores (Fig. 16d).

Photocatalytic activity of COFs makes them excellent candidates for antibacterial photocatalytic therapy [286]. However, in comparison with inorganic semiconductors, COFs have a relatively high exciton binding energy [287]. To address this issue, C-dots as electron extractant were encapsulated in COFs to improve the photocatalytic antibacterial activity of COFs [288]. This system was based on intramolecular charge transfer system to extract π-electrons from photosensitive donor-acceptor COF semiconductors to C-dots through H-bonding. The presence of C-dots significantly improved the charge separation efficiency in COFs by extracting photogenerated π-electrons from COF, leading to the production of more reactive oxygen species. Fabrication of COF from in 4,4′,4′′-(1,3,5-triazine-2,4,6-triyl)trianiline (TTA) as the donor and 1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol (Tp) as the acceptor and then encapsulating 4 wt% C-dots lead to the cell viability <5% against E. coli whereas the cell viability for neat COF was around 43% indicating >8 times increase in the antibacterial activity of COFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent grafting of C-dots to the COF structure
ce:section.ce:para: Surface of D-dots can be easily modified by different types of functional groups, at the same time COFs which are made from organic molecules contain different functional groups which make their modification an easy task. Therefore, fabrication of composite materials by directly covalent attachment between COF and C-dots can further increase the stability of the corresponding composites. A molecularly imprinted network structure as a selective and rapid fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of 4-ethylguaiacol was fabricated by grafting of aminated‑carbon dots to the surface of the COF through imine formation in the presence of 4-ethylguaiacol (4-EG) as the imprinting agent [289]. The COF segment with aldehyde units on its surface was fabricated from triformylphloroglucinol (TP) with p-phenylenediamine (PA). The unreacted amino groups on the surface of carbon dots could create strong acid-base interaction with the phenolic OH group of 4-EG. Indeed, the blue fluorescence of carbon dots could be quenched through acid-based charge transfer interaction. Due to this acid-base charge transfer, this optosensor could detect 4-EG in the linear range of 0.025 to 1 μg/mL with detection limit of 17 ng/mL. Importantly, while 4-EG could change the fluorescence intensity of this probe, negligible changes was observed in the presence of four other structural analogues including p-cresol, 4-ethylphenol, maltol, and 4-vinylguaiacol indicating high selectivity of the probe toward 4-EG. Using a similar strategy another optosensor was developed for detection of tryptamine [290]. In this case, the corresponding molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) were prepared by radical copolymerization of acrylamide, methacrylic acid and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate in the presence of COF/carbon dots and tryptamine as the template molecule. After removing of template tryptamine molecules, the remaining system could be used as a selective sensor for detection of tryptamine. Conjugation between NH2 group of acrylamide with aldehyde groups of COF could stabilize the COF in the MIP framework. The strong acid-base interaction between amino group of tryptamine with COOH group of methacrylic acid could quench the blue fluorescence of carbon dots through charge transfer. This optosensor could detect tryptamine in the range 0.025–0.4 mg kg−1 with a detection limit of 7 μg kg−1 and could be successfully used for detecting tryptamine in meat samples. An ionic-liquid-stabilized fluorescent probe for detection of histamine was developed from an S-doped carbon dot system [291]. Doping the carbon dots by sulfur can increase the fluorescence intensity [292]. Surface functionalization of S-doped carbon dots by methacrylate groups followed by mixing with COF (prepared from 1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol and 2,5-dimethyl-p-phenylenediamine), acrylamide, 1-vinyl-3-butylimidazole tetrafluoroborate, and histidine and then performing the free radical copolymerization produce the targeted imprinted network for histidine detection [291]. Grafting between NH2 groups of acrylamide with aldehyde groups of COF from one side and grafting between CC bonds of carbon dots with other monomers in the medium from the other side generate the targeted sensor. This probe could rapidly and selectively detect histamine in wine and meat products because histamine could quench the blue fluorescence of carbon dots in this system. The obtained probe displayed high stability to acids, bases, and boiling water and showed a detection limit of 5.3 μg kg−1 with a linear range of 10–1000 μg kg−1.

While most COFs are prepared from small organic molecules, in an innovative strategy, Chen et al. developed a simple strategy for fabrication of COFs directly from C-dots (Fig. 17a) [293]. First, C-dots bearing aldehyde moieties with size of around 5 nm were prepared from glutaraldehyde through solvothermal method. Post-condensation of these C-dots with p-phenylenediamine produce the C-dot-based COF hybrid material. Further grafting of the unreacted aldehyde units of C-dots with PEG-NH2 could improve the solubility of the corresponding COF in aqueous medium. Using boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) as diamine produced a biocompatible COF capable of generating reactive oxygen species (ROS). Therefore, it was used as a photodynamic therapy (PDT) agent for tumor treatment. As can be seen in Fig. 17b-d, comparative treatment of mice bearing tumor in right lower leg showed COF in the presence of laser irradiation can effectively kill the tumor cells through ROS formation without affecting the body weight of mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of C-dots/MOF and C-dots/COF composites
ce:section.ce:para: Researchers have shown great interest in the development of C-dots/MOF and C-dots/COF composites due to their promising potential for various applications. However, these composites possess both advantages and disadvantages. C-dots/COF hybrids typically exhibit enhanced thermal and chemical stability compared to their C-dots/MOF counterparts, primarily due to the presence of robust covalent bonds. Additionally, the absence of metal ions in C-dots/COF composites renders them significantly more biologically compatible, thereby enhancing their suitability for a wide range of medical and biomedical applications. However, the presence of metal ions increase the catalytic and photocatalytic activity of C-dots/MOF compared to C-dots/COF. Furthermore, usually C-dots/MOF have more hydrophilic structures compared to C-dots/COF make them better candidates for environment and water treatment applications.

In this review, a detailed overview is provided on the strategies utilized for incorporating fluorescent C-dots into MOF and COF, which are well-known porous materials. These composites have found extensive applications in various fields including sensing, medical, environmental, and energy-related sectors. Practically, MOF and COF are the host of C-dots. The inclusion of C-dots into these hosts can be achieved through either physical encapsulation or covalent conjugation.

The integration of C-dots within the MOF or COF matrices hinders the aggregation of C-dots and enhances their stability in various environments, thereby extending their utility for prolonged periods. Meanwhile, the embedded C-dots act as electron receptors and photosensitizers in these composites, granting them fluorescent properties and increased conductivity. The synergistic effects may boost the effectiveness of these composites in a range of different applications.

In terms of sensing applications, the porous structures of MOF or COF play a crucial role by initially enriching and accumulating the targeted analytes. Moreover, the C-dot/MOF or C-dot/COF composites, which possess multi-emission features, can significantly enhance the precision in quantification and the ability to resist interference. Consequently, C-dot/MOF and C-dot/COF materials exhibit great potential as sensory materials for a wide range of target analytes, encompassing metal ions, humidity, temperature, as well as various organic molecules like biomarkers, drugs, and organic pollutants.

In relation to various electrochemical processes, the porous nature of the hosts enhances the diffusion rate of electrolytes and other resulting products from/to the electrode's surface, while the conductivity of C-dots contributes to a system with reduced overpotential. As a result, these composites are utilized in various electrochemical processes, including the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), ethanol oxidation, and water splitting. In the realm of photocatalyst applications, the inclusion of COF and MOF hosts serves to diminish the occurrence of charge recombination in C-dots, thereby amplifying the efficacy of solar energy utilization.

Extensive research has been conducted on the hybridization of C-dots with MOF hosts; however, due to certain limitations of MOFs as hosts, the hybridization of C-dots with COF counterparts has begun to gain momentum in recent years. The absence of metal within the composition of COF enhances its biocompatibility compared to MOF, rendering the resulting C-dot/COF more appropriate for medical and biomedical applications. Moreover, the hydrophobic nature of COF, coupled with the existence of exceptionally stable covalent bonds within COF, renders the resultant C-dot/COF more suitable for utilization in aqueous environments, particularly in acidic conditions. This is in contrast to MOF, which exhibits inferior stability owing to its weaker coordination bonds. Conversely, the absence of metal active sites in COFs can have detrimental consequences. This includes a decrease in the catalytic and electrochemical performance of C-dot/COF composites.

Both MOF and COF exhibit certain limitations that restrict the full utilization of their respective composites with C-dots. It is possible that the efficacy of C-dot/COF composites can be enhanced through the introduction of metal species as dopants. On the other hand, the stability of C-dot/MOF composites can be augmented by hydrophobization or the incorporation of ligands that possess a strong coordination affinity with the metal center, thereby ensuring stability in aqueous environments.

An alternative approach to addressing the limitations of individual MOF or COF hosts involves creating a bridge between MOF and COF to serve as the host for C-dots. In recent years, there has been a growing focus on the development of Metal-Covalent Organic Frameworks (MCOFs) for applications in catalysis, gas adsorption, and energy storage [294,295]. In this emerging family of frameworks, metal clusters of MOFs are used as building blocks and form the COF structures through dynamic covalent bonds. MCOFs exhibit remarkable stability as COFs and encompass active metal sites similar to those found in MOFs. Hence, the utilization of MCOFs as hosts for C-dots potentially resolves the issues associated with individual COF or MOF hosts, thereby enabling the creation of composites with heightened performance.

Yuqian Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision. Yang Huang: Conceptualization. Huining Xiao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
